Healthcare on the brink: navigating the challenges of an aging society in the United States
===========================================================================================

*   [Charles H. Jones](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#auth-Charles_H_-Jones-Aff1)  [ORCID: orcid.org/0009-0004-0368-6463](http://orcid.org/0009-0004-0368-6463)[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#Aff1) &

[_npj Aging_](https://www.nature.com/npjamd) **volume 10**, Article number: 22 (2024) [Cite this article](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#citeas)

Abstract
--------

The US healthcare system is at a crossroads. With an aging population requiring more care and a strained system facing workforce shortages, capacity issues, and fragmentation, innovative solutions and policy reforms are needed. This paper aims to spark dialogue and collaboration among healthcare stakeholders and inspire action to meet the needs of the aging population. Through a comprehensive analysis of the impact of an aging society, this work highlights the urgency of addressing this issue and the importance of restructuring the healthcare system to be more efficient, equitable, and responsive.

Introduction
------------

The United States is undergoing a demographic and health transformation that will have profound implications for its healthcare system and society. The population is aging at an unprecedented rate, with the baby boomer generation, defined as those born between 1946 and 1964, reaching retirement age and living longer than ever before. According to the U.S. Census Bureau, by 2030, all baby boomers will be older than 65, leading to about one in every five residents being retirement age[1](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR1 "Bureau, U. C. Older Population and Aging. United States Census Bureau 
https://www.census.gov/topics/population/older-aging.html
(2023)."). This shift poses a dual challenge for the healthcare system: how to meet the increasing and complex healthcare needs of the elderly, and how to ensure that the system is prepared and equipped to provide quality and equitable care for this growing segment of the population.

The implications of this demographic shift are far-reaching. The healthcare system, as currently structured, is underprepared for the onslaught of demands this aging population will impose[2](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR2 "Chamie, J., Berkman, L., Hayutin, A. M., Smith, J. P. & Hoeksema, M. J. Why Population Ageing Matters: A Global Perspectives (Center for Migration Studies, 2007)."). The system is also fragmented, inefficient, and costly, with gaps in coordination, quality, and access. Moreover, the system does not adequately address the social determinants of health, such as income, education, housing, transportation, and social support, that impact the health outcomes and health behaviors of the older adults[3](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR3 "CDC. Equitably Addressing Social Determinants of Health and Chronic Diseases, CDC 
https://www.cdc.gov/chronicdisease/healthequity/social-determinants-of-health-and-chronic-disease.html?CDC_AA_refVal=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.cdc.gov%2Fchronicdisease%2Fprograms-impact%2Fsdoh.htm
(2022)."),[4](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR4 "Lopes, L., Montero, A., Presiado, M., Liz H. Americans’ Challenges with Health Care Costs. KFF 
https://www.kff.org/health-costs/issue-brief/americans-challenges-with-health-care-costs/
(2023)."),[5](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR5 "Chetty, R. et al. The association between income and life expectancy in the United States, 2001-2014. JAMA 315, 1750–1766 (2016)."). Nor does it sufficiently engage the older adults and their caregivers in the planning and delivery of care or leverage the potential of community-based and home-based care models, which can improve the access, quality, and affordability of care for the elderly[6](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR6 "He, Y., Wei, B. & Li, Y. The impact of using community home-based elderly care services on older adults’ self-reported health: fresh evidence from China. Front. Public Health 11, 1257463 (2023).").

However, the challenge does not end there. There is a growing shortage of healthcare providers, which means that the supply and availability of qualified and skilled healthcare professionals, such as physicians, nurses, pharmacists, clinical social workers and technicians, is insufficient and inadequate to meet the demand and need of the population. This phenomenon is even more pronounced in low- and middle-income countries, as well as in the rural and remote areas within the U.S. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), the global health workforce was 43.5 million in 2018, and is projected to grow to 53.9 million by 2030, but still falls short of the estimated demand of 80 million by 2030, resulting in a global shortfall of 18 million health workers, mostly in low- and middle-income countries[7](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR7 "WHO. Global Health Workforce statistics database. WHO 
https://www.who.int/data/gho/data/themes/topics/health-workforce
(2023)."). In the U.S., a study by the Association of American Medical Colleges (AAMC) predicts a shortage of up to 139,000 physicians by 2033[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR8 "Dall, T. et al. The Complexities of Physician Supply and Demand: Projections From 2018 to 2033. AAMC 
https://www.aamc.org/system/files/2020-06/stratcomm-aamc-physician-workforce-projections-june-2020.pdf
(2020)."). The shortage of healthcare providers has significant implications for the health sector, as it affects the access, quality, and cost of healthcare, as well as the health outcomes and satisfaction of the population.

As such, the U.S. is facing a healthcare paradox[9](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR9 "Gurwitz, J. H. The paradoxical decline of geriatric medicine as a profession. JAMA 330, 693–694 (2023)."). On one side, there is an aging population with increasing healthcare needs, and on the other, there is a strained healthcare system grappling with workforce shortages, capacity challenges, and fragmentation. Addressing this paradox requires innovative solutions, policy reforms, and a commitment to restructuring the healthcare system to be more efficient, equitable, and responsive to the needs of its aging citizens.

Although many of these issues may be front-of-mind for geriatrics specialists, many other stakeholders in the eldercare system likely do not understand the full scale of challenges brought on by a rapidly aging populace or may underestimate their preparedness for the resulting changes. This was seen in a recent survey of stakeholders in adult vaccine market, which represents a sector critical in the eldercare industry[10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR10 "Jones, C. H., Jenkins, M. P., Adam Williams, B., Welch, V. L. & True, J. M. Exploring the future adult vaccine landscape—crowded schedules and new dynamics. npj Vaccines 9, 27 (2024)."). When presented with the projected rise of adult vaccines, a trend driven largely by the needs of the aging population, stakeholders across the market were unaware of the associated complexities and anticipated minimal challenges in adopting expanded vaccine schedules[10](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR10 "Jones, C. H., Jenkins, M. P., Adam Williams, B., Welch, V. L. & True, J. M. Exploring the future adult vaccine landscape—crowded schedules and new dynamics. npj Vaccines 9, 27 (2024)."). While vaccines represent only a portion of the eldercare market, it is reasonable to believe that this lack of a holistic understanding applies to all sectors in the eldercare industry.

This paper aims to stimulate dialogue and collaboration among the healthcare stakeholders, and to inspire action and innovation to address the needs and aspirations of the aging population. To accomplish this, this work will provide a comprehensive and critical analysis of the impact and implications of an aging society to highlight the importance and urgency of this issue.

Rising tide of healthcare needs: increasing demand and complexity of care
-------------------------------------------------------------------------

The U.S. population is aging rapidly because of two interrelated factors: the aging of the baby boomer generation, and the increase in life expectancy. The baby boomer generation constitutes the largest cohort in the U.S. history, with about 73 million members[11](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR11 "Staff, A. C. 2020 Census Will Help Policymakers Prepare for the Incoming Wave of Aging Boomers. United States Census Bureau 
https://www.census.gov/library/stories/2019/12/by-2030-all-baby-boomers-will-be-age-65-or-older.html#:~:text=The%202020%20Census%20will%20provide,on%20America’s%20population%20age%20structure.%E2%80%9D&text=Born%20after%20World%20War%20II,will%20turn%2074%20next%20year
(2019)."). As this cohort reaches retirement age, the share of the population that is 65 and older will increase significantly, from 17% in 2022 to 21% in 2030, and to 23% in 2050 (Fig. [1A](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#Fig1))[12](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR12 "2023 National Population Projections Tables: Main Series. United States Census Bureau 
https://www.census.gov/data/tables/2023/demo/popproj/2023-summary-tables.html
(2023)."). By 2050, the number of Americans aged 65 and older will increase by 40%, from 58 million in 2022 to 82 million in 2050.

**Fig. 1: Growth of senior population and chronic disease burden.**

[![Image 6: figure 1](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41514-024-00148-2/MediaObjects/41514_2024_148_Fig1_HTML.png)](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2/figures/1)

This figure illustrates the expected growth of the senior population and the associated increase in chronic disease burden. Raw data sourced from “2023 National Population Projections Tables: Main Series”, which utilizes official estimates of resident population on July 1, 2022 as the base for projecting the U.S. population from 2023 to 2100 (panel **a**) and Boersma, et al., “Prevalence of Multiple Chronic Conditions Among U.S. Adults, 2018” (panel **b**).

[Full size image](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2/figures/1)

The aging population, particularly those over 85 years old, presents new challenges for the medical system. This will be the fastest growing segment, tripling in size from 6.5 million in 2022 to 17.3 million in 2050, a number comparable to the current population of New York state[13](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR13 "QuickFacts: New York. United States Census Bureau 
https://www.census.gov/quickfacts/fact/table/NY/PST045223
(2023)."). These elderly adults often suffer from multiple and complex health conditions, including age-related diseases that affect their heart, brain, and immune system. However, the medical system lacks the experience and expertise to effectively treat these diseases and provide specialized, personalized care for this vulnerable group. The increase in the share and size of the older population will have implications for the demand and supply of healthcare and social services, as well as for the economic and fiscal stability of the nation.

One of the main drivers of the increased healthcare demand and utilization among the elderly is the high prevalence of multiple chronic conditions (MCCs), which are defined as having two or more chronic diseases that last at least a year and require ongoing medical attention or limit activities of daily living[14](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR14 "Davis-Ajami, M. L., Lu, Z. K. & Wu, J. Multiple chronic conditions and associated health care expenses in US adults with cancer: a 2010–2015 Medical Expenditure Panel Survey study. BMC Health Serv. Res. 19, 1–10 (2019)."),[15](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR15 "Goodman, R. A., Posner, S. F., Huang, E. S., Parekh, A. K. & Koh, H. K. Peer reviewed: defining and measuring chronic conditions: imperatives for research, policy, program, and practice. Prev. Chronic Dis. 10, E66 (2013)."). According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), 88% of older adults have at least one MCC, and 60% have at least two (Fig. [1B](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#Fig1)). These include common conditions such as hypertension, arthritis, heart disease, cancer, diabetes, and chronic kidney disease. MCCs are associated with increased mortality, disability, functional decline, and reduced quality of life. Moreover, they pose significant challenges to healthcare provision and management, as they require complex and coordinated care across multiple settings and providers. A study by Machlin et al. (2019) found that among Medicare beneficiaries aged 65 and older, those with MCCs accounted for 94% of total healthcare expenditures in 2010, compared to 6% for those without MCCs[16](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR16 "Machlin, S. R. & Soni, A. Health care expenditures for adults with multiple treated chronic conditions: estimates from the Medical Expenditure Panel Survey, 2009. Prev. Chronic Dis. 10, E63 (2013)."). The average annual expenditure per person was $21,342 for those with four or more MCCs, $13,272 for those with three MCCs, $9176 for those with two MCCs, and $5865 for those with one MCC. These figures contrast sharply with the $2025 spent for those without any MCC.

The high prevalence of MCCs among the elderly is expected to persist or even increase in the future, as it is closely linked to the increase in life expectancy. As people live longer, they are more likely to develop and accumulate chronic diseases over time, especially if they have risk factors such as age-related physiological changes, environmental exposures, lifestyle behaviors, genetic predispositions, and social determinants of health. For example, a study by Crimmins and Beltrán-Sánchez[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR17 "Crimmins, E. M. & Beltrán-Sánchez, H. Mortality and morbidity trends: is there compression of morbidity? J. Gerontol. B: Psychol. Sci. Soc. Sci. 66, 75–86 (2011).") found that the increase in life expectancy in the U.S. between 1998 and 2008 was accompanied by an increase in the number of years spent with MCCs, especially among the elderly[17](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR17 "Crimmins, E. M. & Beltrán-Sánchez, H. Mortality and morbidity trends: is there compression of morbidity? J. Gerontol. B: Psychol. Sci. Soc. Sci. 66, 75–86 (2011)."). The study estimated that the average number of years spent with MCCs increased from 7.2 to 8.6 for men aged 65 and older, and from 10.0 to 11.3 for women aged 65 and older. Therefore, the aging population will face a higher burden of chronic diseases and a lower quality of life in the coming decades. Making matters worse, the health and longevity of the next wave of aging people may also be affected by new external triggers, such as obesity, processed food intake, microbiome changes, climate change, pandemics, and pollution, which can have diverse and unpredictable impacts on different individuals. These triggers can also change the health behaviors and healthcare access of the elderly.

Another challenge that arises from the medication requirements of the aging population is polypharmacy, which is defined as the concurrent use of five or more medications[18](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR18 "Masnoon, N., Shakib, S., Kalisch-Ellett, L. & Caughey, G. E. What is polypharmacy? A systematic review of definitions. BMC Geriatr. 17, 1–10 (2017)."). Individuals aged 65 and over account for over a third of all prescribed medications in the U.S.[19](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR19 "Cohen, R. A. & Boersma, P. Strategies used by adults aged 65 and over to reduce their prescription drug costs, 2016-2017. NCHS Data Brief 335, 1–8 (2019)."). However, polypharmacy can have negative consequences, such as increased risk of drug interactions, adverse drug events, medication non-adherence, and medication errors. These can lead to poor outcomes, such as reduced effectiveness, increased morbidity and mortality, and decreased quality of life. Therefore, polypharmacy necessitates careful medication management and monitoring, as well as regular medication reviews and deprescribing when appropriate. A study by Qato et al. [20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR20 "Qato, D. M., Wilder, J., Schumm, L. P., Gillet, V. & Alexander, G. C. Changes in prescription and over-the-counter medication and dietary supplement use among older adults in the United States, 2005 vs 2011. JAMA Intern. Med. 176, 473–482 (2016).") found that among U.S. adults aged 65 and older, the prevalence of polypharmacy increased from 31.4% in 1999-2000 to 35.8% in 2011-2012, and the prevalence of potentially inappropriate medication use increased from 8.7% to 10.0%[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR20 "Qato, D. M., Wilder, J., Schumm, L. P., Gillet, V. & Alexander, G. C. Changes in prescription and over-the-counter medication and dietary supplement use among older adults in the United States, 2005 vs 2011. JAMA Intern. Med. 176, 473–482 (2016)."). The study also found that polypharmacy was associated with higher rates of emergency department visits and hospitalizations[20](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR20 "Qato, D. M., Wilder, J., Schumm, L. P., Gillet, V. & Alexander, G. C. Changes in prescription and over-the-counter medication and dietary supplement use among older adults in the United States, 2005 vs 2011. JAMA Intern. Med. 176, 473–482 (2016).").

The economic implications of the aging population for the healthcare sector are profound, as they affect not only the healthcare spending and resource utilization, but also the healthcare workforce, the healthcare quality, and the healthcare innovation. According to the Congressional Budget Office, the federal spending on major health programs for the elderly, such as Medicare and Medicaid, will increase from 6.6% of gross domestic product (GDP) in 2020 to 9.2% of GDP in 2050[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR21 "The 2020 Long-Term Budget Outlook. Congressional Budget Office 
https://www.cbo.gov/system/files/2020-09/56516-LTBO.pdf
(2020)."). This projected growth is presumably driven by the older age segments differing healthcare utilization patterns and the increasing complexity of care. Specifically, it is estimated that adults aged 65+ visits doctors 20% more frequently than younger adults and experience a threefold increase in hospitalization rates[22](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR22 "Health, United States, 2014: With Special Feature on Adults Aged 55–64. National Center for Health Statistics 
https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/data/hus/hus14.pdf
(2015)."). This increased utilization and complexity of care drives increased spending as reported in a 2019 Kaiser Family Foundation study that found Medicare spending for beneficiaries aged 65 to 74 averages $7566, which nearly doubles to $16,145 for those aged 85 and older[23](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR23 "Cubanski, J., Neuman T., Griffin, S., and Damico, A. Medicare Spending at the End of Life: A Snapshot of Beneficiaries Who Died in 2014 and the Cost of Their Care. KFF 
https://www.kff.org/medicare/issue-brief/medicare-spending-at-the-end-of-life/
(2016).").

The rising tide of healthcare needs due to an aging population is multifaceted, encompassing increased service utilization, higher prevalence of chronic diseases, escalated healthcare spending, and complex medication management. This scenario places unprecedented demands on the healthcare system, calling for innovative approaches in care delivery, financial planning, and resource allocation. Adapting to these changes requires a concerted effort from healthcare providers, policymakers, and stakeholders to ensure that the system is not only responsive but also sustainable in meeting the evolving needs of an aging society. Therefore, it is imperative to recognize and discuss the impact this evolution will have on the demand for healthcare professionals, such as physicians, nurses, pharmacists, and specialists in geriatrics, who will be the critical in providing adequate and appropriate care for the elderly with complex medical needs.

The overburdened healthcare landscape: healthcare delivery challenges
---------------------------------------------------------------------

The demand for healthcare workers is expected to outpace the supply, resulting in a projected deficit of 1.2 million registered nurses and 121,900 physicians by 2030[8](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR8 "Dall, T. et al. The Complexities of Physician Supply and Demand: Projections From 2018 to 2033. AAMC 
https://www.aamc.org/system/files/2020-06/stratcomm-aamc-physician-workforce-projections-june-2020.pdf
(2020)."),[21](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41514-024-00148-2#ref-CR21 "The 2020 Long-Term Budget Outlook. Congressional Budget Office

INTRODUCTION
------------

When Queen Elizabeth II was crowned in 1952, life expectancy was 66 years for males and 71 years for females. By 2001, these figures were 77 and 81 respectively. Average life expectancy at 70 is already 17 years for males and 19 years for females. Old age is often caricatured as a time of ill-health, loneliness, unhappiness, and dependence, but life satisfaction peaks in the 70s and most over-80s rate their health as being good or excellent and say they do not live with life-limiting long-term conditions (LTCs). Most over 65s are neither disabled nor dependent.[1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3404305/#b1)

Despite this good news, ageing does pose significant challenges to health and social care systems, which need to change to meet radically different patterns of demand. I believe there are at least 10 key challenges for general practice in providing care for an ageing population. Several are well-illustrated by articles in this welcome themed issue of the _BJGP_.

MEETING THE EFFICIENCY CHALLENGE
--------------------------------

In England, people over 65 years comprise 46% of spend in acute care, 37% in primary care, 60% in social care, 60% of admissions, and 70% of bed days in hospital. Many older patients have complex needs or use multiple services. There is major unwarranted variation between primary care trusts in rates of emergency hospital admission or bed utilisation in over 65s; and in a range of disease-specific processes and outcomes.[2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3404305/#b2) We need to focus more on older people with multiple morbidities, whose care should be more prominent in plans for service redesign.

IMPROVING QUALITY AND COMBATING DISCRIMINATION
----------------------------------------------

Older patients often get a poor deal in the NHS and other systems relative to other age-groups.[3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3404305/#b3) Common age-related conditions receive lower priority and older people receive generally lower quality of care than those in midlife with the same diseases. Frailer old people who present with so-called ‘non-specific’ or ‘atypical’ syndromes like immobility, falls, confusion, or failure to thrive are often written off as ‘social’ or ‘acopia’ instead of being accurately assessed and diagnosed; despite these presentations being entirely typical and the benefits of comprehensive geriatric assessment well-evidenced.[4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3404305/#b4) Ageist attitudes or poor communication with older patients and their carers are common. All clinicians, including GPs, need to tackle these; whether through education, training, regulation, legislation, or re-balancing system or contract incentives.

A GREATER FOCUS ON PREVENTION
-----------------------------

Most health and social care systems are seen as too reactive to crises or high levels of need, rather than preventing demand and preserving health.[5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3404305/#b5) We need to ‘invert the triangle’, focusing more on prevention across the whole life course and the various stages of old age. Prevention can be ‘primary’, preventing the onset of LTCs, frailty, or disability (with some of the broader solutions lying in communities, housing, or technology rather than health services); ‘secondary’, helping those with these problems to remain well; and ‘tertiary’, ensuring that when people do suffer acute crises or complications they recover well. General practice is a key player in all three. Drewes _et al_ illustrate these challenges, exploring GPs’ views on prevention for more active older patients and those with frailty or disability for whom preventing functional decline and maintaining independence is the key goal.[6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3404305/#b6)

PROACTIVE CARE OF OLDER PEOPLE WITH MULTIPLE LTCs
-------------------------------------------------

There is a growing focus in health policy on risk stratification, proactive management, care planning, case-management, and tele-health and, for patients with LTCs, with ‘anticipatory care’, key to preventing deterioration, keeping patients well, independent and out of hospitals, or long-term care.[7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3404305/#b7) In England, there is ongoing work on developing whole year of care payments for patients with LTCs based on level of need rather than disease, with shared risk between primary and secondary care. However, some factors have been missing from many local LTC plans. First, most patients over 75 years have at least three LTCs, so that a ‘single-disease’ approach featuring the ‘usual suspects’ (for example, diabetes, respiratory, or cardiac disease) is not fit for purpose.[8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3404305/#b8),[9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3404305/#b9) LTC care needs to shift to older people with multiple comorbidities. Second, those ‘usual suspects’ tend to ignore common and costly conditions associated with ageing, such as dementia, incontinence, mobility problems, and bone fragility. Third, they rarely feature frailty syndrome or disability.

ADDRESSING THE UNTOLD STORIES OF FRAILTY AND AGE-RELATED DISABILITY
-------------------------------------------------------------------

Most disabled people are over 65: 90% of those registered blind or partially sighted are over 65, and around 40% of over 65-year olds have significant hearing impairment. While only around 15% of over 65s in England and Wales are disabled, and only half of these are dependent on others for care, these older people account disproportionately for healthcare use and hospitalisation. Frailty syndrome can be characterised as ‘poor functional reserve’ or defined by weight loss, muscle weakness, slow walking speed, and fatigability. Although frailty only affects an estimated 6% of people over 65, it features highly in resource utilisation: as it underlies the so called ‘geriatric syndromes’ of falls, immobility, delirium, or incontinence which account for so much bed occupancy in acute hospitals, reliance on home care services, and often triggers the move to long-term care. There are overlaps between LTC management and the care of the oldest old, but they are not completely synonymous.

DEALING WITH DEMENTIA
---------------------

There are already an estimated 800 000 people in Britain with dementia, projected to double over the next two decades. Dementia has been estimated to cost more to society than heart disease, stroke, and cancer combined.[10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3404305/#b10) It complicates many other morbidities, so that one in four adult hospital beds is occupied by someone with dementia — with half never diagnosed before admission to hospital in crisis — and around two-thirds of long-term care residents have dementia or cognitive impairment. The Dementia Strategy in England has prioritised earlier diagnosis and support, which has divided opinion among GPs about potential benefits and harms. Here Phillips _et al_ present a fascinating exploration of barriers to dementia diagnosis among GPs;[11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3404305/#b11) next, perhaps, an exploration of interventions to overcome them?

CRISIS INTERVENTION AND RAPID RESPONSE
--------------------------------------

In addition to a greater focus on proactive care, there is also a need for rapid response to crises and supportive early interventions. Frail older patients are often unable to attend a surgery. They are also often seen in crisis by doctors from out-of-hours services who may be less familiar with their cases. Community nurses see many older people at home, but still value ready access to medical support. The role of the home visit for older patients may be important in service redesign. In this issue, Van Kempen _et al_ spell out just how much older people and their families value and benefit from home visits.[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3404305/#b12) With the changing nature of general practice, is it too late to turn back the clock? Very short consultations do not allow for the kind of comprehensive assessment and support that the patients described here rate so highly.

MEDICATION
----------

The rising prevalence of LTCs with age inevitably means that long-term prescriptions and polypharmacy are highest in older people, as are potential drug–drug or drug–disease interaction, non-adherence, and iatrogenesis. Incentives such as the Quality and Outcomes Framework, which has done much to drive quality, have also probably led to over-prescribing in our oldest and frailest patients and done too little to drive medication cessation. Articles by Geerts _et al_[13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3404305/#b13) and by McCann _et al_[14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3404305/#b14) illustrate the challenges, describing interventions to improve prescription and monitoring of medications in ‘at risk’ groups, of older patients with chronic kidney disease and visual impairment respectively.

CARE IN NURSING HOMES
---------------------

A relatively small proportion of our over 65s are in nursing and residential homes. But they are among the sickest and most complex patients, with multiple comorbidities, disability, a high prevalence of dementia, mobility, or continence problems, and often harmful polypharmacy.[15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3404305/#b15) Residents too often end up being admitted to hospital as unplanned emergencies, sometimes avoidably and sometimes for end-of-life care. Older people in long-term care have much worse access to primary care and a range of community health services than those in other settings, and their care is too often reactive rather than proactive. We need concerted efforts to tackle this problem.[16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3404305/#b16) McDermott _et al_ describe GPs’ views on their role in nursing homes and the wider factors that impact on their ability to avoid hospital admissions for residents.[17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3404305/#b17)

INTEGRATION, CONTINUITY, AND SYSTEM LEADERSHIP
----------------------------------------------

Finally, in systems such as the NHS, where primary, secondary, social, mental health, and community health services usually have separate accountabilities, responsibilities, and information systems, patients may suffer care that lacks coordination and continuity. They often feel bewildered about ‘who is who’, find they have to repeat the same information to multiple professionals, and suffer errors and breakdowns of communication at the interfaces between agencies. Making care ‘joined-up’ has benefits for whole systems as well as for individuals, and it is crucial in considering older people that we think about every aspect of the care pathway, GPs need to be in the vanguard of driving this change as clinicians and as leaders as they have the best overview of the ‘end to end’ care pathway and the biggest stake in getting it right. This will require a major shift of focus from traditional ways of working both for GPs and their hospital colleagues, but population ageing is such a ‘game changer’ for services that we cannot continue as we are.

Abstract
--------

[Aging populations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/ageing-population "Learn more about Aging populations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") exert upwards pressure on healthcare systems, raising concerns about increasing expenditures on health. This paper reviews the empirical literature on the issue and critically assesses the strengths and [weaknesses](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/nursing-and-health-professions/weakness "Learn more about weaknesses from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the outcomes measured, methodologies used, and the hypotheses tested. While age strongly predicts long-term care expenditure, the time-to-death factor renders the aging effect null for hospital care expenditure. Existing literature disagrees on the importance of age and time-to-death effects on prescription drug and [ambulatory care](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/nursing-and-health-professions/ambulatory-care "Learn more about ambulatory care from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") costs. Morbidity and medical innovation mediate these effects, proving crucial for aging-related healthcare expenditure growth. We identify several opportunities for future research including [gender differences](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/nursing-and-health-professions/gender-difference "Learn more about gender differences from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), utilization of emerging methods, and the importance of institutional settings.

Introduction
------------

In recent decades, healthcare expenditures have displayed a continuous upward trajectory in many countries ([Getzen, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b39), [Getzen, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b40)), both in terms of per capita expenditures and as a percentage of GDP. This persistent trend has instigated concerns that healthcare expenditures will perpetually escalate ([Mason and Miller, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b61)), thereby posing a threat to the fiscal stability of healthcare systems. Coinciding with the surge in healthcare spending, many developed nations have grappled with the challenge of an [aging population](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/ageing-population "Learn more about aging population from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Lee, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b57)), i.e., a higher proportion of older individuals ([Sanderson and Scherbov, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b81)). This demographic shift is particularly concerning due to the well-established positive correlation between age and healthcare expenditures ([Bakx et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b11), [Fledsberg et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b33)). Consequently, population aging has been identified as a pivotal factor contributing to the present and anticipated future growth in healthcare expenditures ([Lee and Miller, 2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b58)).

A crucial discovery in this context was the identification of time-to-death as a critical determinant of healthcare expenditures ([Lubitz and Riley, 1993](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b60)), leading to the formulation of the Red Herring Hypothesis. The hypothesis posits that aging itself does not substantially impact healthcare expenditures when adjusted for an individual’s proximity to death ([Zweifel et al., 1999](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b98)). The widespread acceptance of this hypothesis ([Seshamani and Gray, 2004a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b83), [Seshamani and Gray, 2004b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b84)) initially provided [reassurance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/reassurance "Learn more about reassurance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for public finance ([Geue et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b41)), shifting the focus from age-related effects of population aging to [cohort effects](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/cohort-effect "Learn more about cohort effects from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and other time trends ([van Baal and Wong, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b87), [Colombier, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b23)). Subsequent research has revealed a disconcerting trend of steepening age-profiles in healthcare expenditures, indicating that average healthcare costs increase more rapidly for older individuals over time ([Buchner and Wasem, 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b16)). This development has posed new challenges, especially in the face of a growing population of elderly individuals. Moreover, a multitude of additional factors has been acknowledged in the effects of aging on healthcare expenditures, including morbidity ([Wong et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b91)), advancements in [medical technology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/medical-technology "Learn more about medical technology from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Chandra and Skinner, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b19), [Laudicella et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b55)), high-end-of-life healthcare expenses ([French et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b37)), and socioeconomic status ([Cookson et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b25), [Christensen et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b21)).

While several excellent reviews have been undertaken in the past, they have generally been devoted to synthesizing the literature either on one particular determinant or a specific type of healthcare service. For instance, [Zweifel (2022)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b97) provides an extensive review of evidence for the Red Herring Hypothesis, while [Balia and Brau (2014)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b12) outline the evidence for long-term care expenditure. Our paper presents a comprehensive narrative review of the health economics literature encompassing a wide range of age-related determinants of healthcare and for various types of healthcare expenditures. Our objectives with this review are twofold. First, we synthesize the findings of previous literature with a particular focus on analyzing existing gaps, pinpointing areas where research is sparse or inconsistent, and identifying key questions that remain unanswered. Second, we propose future directions for achieving significant advancements beyond the current state-of-the-art. Section “Search strategy and selection criteria” outlines our search strategy and selection criteria for this narrative review which, is designed to be a valuable resource not only for health economists, [epidemiologists](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/nursing-and-health-professions/epidemiologist "Learn more about epidemiologists from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and policymakers but also for scholars from various disciplines interested in aging studies.

Trends in aging and healthcare expenditures
-------------------------------------------

### Trends in aging

During the past century, there has been a significant increase in life expectancy across the developed world ([Oeppen and Vaupel, 2002](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b70), [Pascariu et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b74)). Previously, life expectancy at birth increased primarily due to a reduction in the risk of dying at young ages. However, in recent decades, mortality reductions have been observed at higher and higher ages ([Rau et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b76)). As a consequence, the risk of dying during post-retirement ages has also trended downwards ([Zuo et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b96)), indicating that the proportions of the population aged 65 and above have been increasing. [Murphy (2017)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b67) attributes 66 percent of the steep increase in the mean age of populations within Western European countries to be mortality driven while decreasing cohort sizes accounted for 32 percent. The remaining 2 percent is attributed to migration.

Recent trends, however, suggest a slowdown in the reduction of [mortality rates](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/nursing-and-health-professions/mortality-rate "Learn more about mortality rates from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Murphy and Grundy, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b68)). From 2011 to 2016, the United Kingdom witnessed one of the most significant slowdowns in life expectancy improvements at birth and at age 65 for both men and women ([Office for National Statistics, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b71)). Furthermore, the COVID-19 pandemic profoundly impacted global mortality, causing a substantial rise in death rates across numerous countries, especially at the height of its outbreaks ([Aburto et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b1), [Schumacher et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b82)). The most severely impacted groups were older adults and individuals with pre-existing health conditions. To what extent these recent developments in mortality will continue, is complex and depends on multiple factors, including [public health policies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/public-health-policy "Learn more about public health policies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), advances in healthcare, socioeconomic conditions, and potential future health crises.

[Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#fig1) illustrates this change in the European Union’s population from 1970 (outlined) to 2019 (colored bars) for males (left) and females (right), using data from the [Human Mortality Database (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b47). In 1970, the population distribution displayed a typical pyramidal shape, with a concentration of individuals aged 0 to around 25 representing the [base](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/base "Learn more about base from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the pyramid, the post-war baby [boomers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/boomers "Learn more about boomers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), a substantial middle-aged group in the middle, and smaller cohorts among those over 60 at the top. The smaller cohorts at ages 51 to 54 in 1970 are a result of low birth rates during World War I ([Vandenbroucke, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b88), [Mitchell, 1998](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b64)). However, over the past 50 years, the [age distribution](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/pharmacology-toxicology-and-pharmaceutical-science/age-distribution "Learn more about age distribution from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the population has undergone a dramatic transformation, resembling the shape of a spinning top by 2019. Baby boomers now represent the largest cohorts, while cohorts below age 25 are relatively smaller. Additionally, the population aged 75 and above has increased. Similar demographic transitions have been observed across high-income countries ([Human Mortality Database, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b47)). This changing age structure is central to concerns about [aging populations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/ageing-population "Learn more about aging populations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and escalating healthcare expenditures, as individuals in older age groups generally incur higher healthcare costs ([Alemayehu and Warner, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b5)).

### Trends in national healthcare expenditures

From 1970 to 2021, national healthcare expenditures displayed upward trends in tandem with the demographic transition discussed in Section “Trends in Aging”. To illustrate these temporal trends, we used [OECD (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b69) country-level data from three high-income countries: the United States, Germany, and Denmark. The left panel of [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#fig2) graphs per capita healthcare expenditures over time, showing a consistent increase for all countries. While starting almost equally in 1970, the United States witnessed the most rapid growth, with Germany also displaying continuous annual increases but at a more modest rate. In comparison, Denmark experienced a growth pattern similar to Germany’s but with periods of limited growth.

Plotting expenditures on health as a proportion of GDP on the right panel of [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#fig2), we notice remarkable differences between the three countries: In USA and Germany, expenditures on health increase as a proportion of GDP, whereas Denmark exhibits fairly constant levels in four out of five decades. Even in periods with strong population aging, expenditures on health can be contained within the politically determined healthcare budgeting of Denmark.

Age and time-to-death effects
-----------------------------

Chronological age is of natural interest in studies of the healthcare expenditure effects of aging, due to the higher average healthcare expenditures of older individuals ([Bakx et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b11), [Fledsberg et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b33)). However, early research observed that age alone may not be inherently significant ([Fuchs, 1984](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b38)).

### Red herring hypothesis

Subsequent studies have shown that time-to-death is a stronger predictor of healthcare expenditures than age ([Lubitz and Riley, 1993](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b60), [Riley and Lubitz, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b78)). This observation has led to the development of the Red Herring Hypothesis, first proposed by [Zweifel et al. (1999)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b98), which argues that time-to-death, rather than age, is the primary predictor of healthcare expenditures. In a [linear regression](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/medicine-and-dentistry/linear-regression-analysis "Learn more about linear regression from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") framework, simplified for ease of exposition, healthcare expenditures, H, for individual, i, are a function of age, A, time-to-death, T T D, and other determinants, X R H, which have effects β=β A,β T T D,β X′′(1)Red Herring:H i=β A A i+β T T D T T D i+β X X i R H.

The null hypothesis in the Red Herring framework is that healthcare expenditures do not change with age once time-to-death is taken into account, ∂H/∂A=β A=0 or, alternatively, the marginal change is _small_ ([Zweifel et al., 1999](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b98)). Meanwhile, the effect of time-to-death, ∂H/∂T T D=β T T D, is large and significant. Often, the function for healthcare expenditures is specified as a two-part model ([Deb and Norton, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b28)) with a logit or a [probit model](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/probit-model "Learn more about probit model from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") as the first part modeling the use of healthcare expenditures and the second part a generalized linear model account for the heavy-tailed distribution of positive healthcare usage, e.g., a Poisson model with a log-link. We discuss the functional specification in Section “Methodological opportunities”.

The Red Herring Hypothesis found empirical support in several subsequent studies using other datasets and empirical approaches. For example, ([Seshamani and Gray, 2004a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b83)) confirmed the relevance of time-to-death in explaining [hospital expenditures](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/nursing-and-health-professions/hospital-running-cost "Learn more about hospital expenditures from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in England, i.e., publicly insured expenditures. They found that as individuals approach death, hospital expenditures significantly increase, starting at £148 with 20 quarters to death and exponentially increasing to £1698 with one quarter to death. In another paper, ([Seshamani and Gray, 2004b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b84)) found that hospital expenditures tend to increase slightly more with time-to-death at higher ages, suggesting that it is generally more costly to die at higher ages. These findings, though modest in size, raise concerns about the healthcare expenditure effects of aging, as healthcare expenditures tend to increase with age.

### Steepening hypothesis

A supplementary hypothesis to the Red Herring Hypothesis was suggested a few years later by [Buchner and Wasem (2006)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b16). The Steepening Hypothesis proposes that the age-profile of expenditures steepens over time, with healthcare expenditures growing at a faster rate over time for older individuals than younger individuals. The Steepening Hypothesis, unlike the Red Herring Hypothesis, excludes time-to-death and instead addresses the relationship between healthcare expenditures across two dimensions, namely chronological age and calendar time. A stylized linear expression with calendar time, T, additional determinants, X S, and parameters δ=δ A,δ T,δ A⋅T,δ X′′ takes the form (2)Steepening:H i=δ A A i+δ T T i+δ A⋅T+δ X X i S.The Steepening Hypothesis posits a positive change in healthcare expenditures with respect to age and time, ∂2 H/∂A∂T=δ A⋅T>0, but places no restrictions on neither age or time effects. Using private healthcare insurance data from Swiss cantons, [Felder and Werblow (2008)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b31) found no support for the hypothesis. However, several years later, [Gregersen (2014)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b42) reexamined the Steepening Hypothesis using Norwegian publicly insured hospital expenditure data, and found evidence to support the hypothesis, as did additional research ([von Wyl, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212828X24000185#b89), [Kjellberg and Ibsen, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/arti
